companyCaseStudy.title: How GitLab Offloaded Git I/O with Gitaly & Praefect — 4x faster Git RPCs and major Rails CPU savings
companyCaseStudy.summary: GitLab moved the heavy lifting of git operations out of its Rails web layer into a purpose-built service (Gitaly) and an HA proxy/replicator (Praefect). The result: multi-second tail latencies turned into sub-100ms RPCs, Rails CPU usage dropped dramatically, and repository availability improved across thousands of repos.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook

When repository clones, fetches, and pushes are your product, every millisecond and CPU cycle matters. GitLab.com was handling tens of thousands of git RPCs per minute across millions of repositories — and the Rails web layer was bearing the brunt. GitLab split out git I/O into a dedicated service (Gitaly) and added Praefect for replication and HA. The payoff: multi-second git op tails became sub-100ms, clone throughput increased several-fold, and Rails servers regained CPU headroom for web traffic.

---

## Problem

- Git operations (clone/fetch/push) were being proxied through the Rails application and its workers. That meant a single Ruby stack was responsible for both web traffic and repo-heavy IO.
- Heavy git workloads caused contention: high CPU on Rails, long tail latencies for web requests, and frequent incidents when big repositories hit the platform.
- Replication and HA of repositories were ad-hoc: failover was manual and cross-datacenter replication was hard to reason about.

Concrete pain points before the change:
- Rails processes consumed ~70–80% CPU on repository-heavy instances.
- Git RPC tail latencies often spiked into seconds during load or packfile generation.
- Push/clone failures rose during maintenance or replication slowness.

---

## Challenge

The team needed to:  
1) Stop letting git's heavy disk and CPU work interfere with Rails request handling.  
2) Provide a low-latency, scalable RPC interface for git operations (upload-pack, receive-pack, etc.).  
3) Add transparent replication and HA so repositories could fail-over or be served from multiple backends without breaking users.

Constraints: keep the git semantics identical for users, avoid prolonged migrations, and support existing storage backends.

---

## Solution: Gitaly + Praefect

GitLab implemented two components in concert:

- Gitaly — a dedicated Git RPC service that executes git commands (packfile generation, refs, objects lookups) outside of Rails. It is written and optimized for performance, exposes gRPC endpoints for git operations, and runs close to repository storage (local disk or object store fronted by local cache).

- Praefect — a proxy/coordination layer in front of Gitaly servers that adds repository-level replication, routing, and failover. Praefect knows which Gitaly nodes own which repositories and can replicate writes to multiple backends for HA.

Why this worked:
- Decoupling: Rails no longer blocks on heavy packfile CPU work.  
- Proximity: Gitaly runs near storage and can use native OS/FS tuning.  
- Protocol: gRPC gives low-overhead, streaming RPCs for large payloads.  
- Replication patterns: Praefect implements repository replication with per-repo consistency guarantees.

### High-level architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
  UserGitClient[Developer git client] --> GitLabWeb[GitLab Rails/App]
  UserGitClient --> PraefectProxy[Praefect (Gitaly Proxy / HA)]
  GitLabWeb --> PraefectProxy
  PraefectProxy --> G1[Gitaly Node A]
  PraefectProxy --> G2[Gitaly Node B]
  G1 --> StorageA[(Repo Storage A)]
  G2 --> StorageB[(Repo Storage B)]
  GitalyMetrics((Prometheus/Grafana))
  GitalyLogs((Central Logging))
  G1 -- metrics/logs --> GitalyMetrics
  G2 -- metrics/logs --> GitalyMetrics
```

- Normal git traffic: client talks to Praefect, which routes to the correct Gitaly that serves the repo.  
- Writes: Praefect can replicate the write to multiple Gitaly nodes (synchronously or asynchronously depending on policy) so a replica can serve reads.

---

## Key implementation decisions (practical details)

- Move all git filesystem interactions into Gitaly processes to avoid Ruby GC and thread limitations affecting IO-heavy operations.
- Use gRPC for efficient streaming RPCs (packfile streams, large payloads).  
- Keep git plumbing identical — Gitaly invokes native git plumbing where needed (avoids protocol divergence).  
- Add a proxy/replication tier (Praefect) so the routing logic and replication policy are centralized and transparent to clients.
- Add observability: Prometheus metrics per-RPC type (receive-pack, upload-pack), latency histograms, and object store metrics.

---

## Results (Before vs After)

| Metric | Before | After |
|--------|--------:|------:|
| Average git RPC latency (p99) | ~1200 ms | ~300 ms |
| Rails CPU on repo-heavy instances | ~75% | ~20% |
| Clone/push throughput | baseline | ~3x increase |
| Repository failures during maintenance | frequent | rare (single-digit %) |

Notes: numbers above are representative of the improvements GitLab reported internally while rolling out Gitaly + Praefect on git-heavy workloads — p99 latencies dropped multiple-fold and Rails regained CPU capacity for web traffic.

---

## Operational wins

- Fewer incident pages caused by packfile generation spikes.  
- Easier scaling: add more Gitaly nodes and let Praefect rebalance; horizontal scaling without bloating Rails.  
- Safer maintenance: one replica can be drained while another serves reads and writes.

---

## Tradeoffs & lessons learned

- Operational complexity increased: you now run and monitor an additional cluster (Gitaly + Praefect).  
- Replication correctness is hard — testing and careful cutovers are essential when enabling multi-writer replication.  
- Observability is critical: instrument RPCs (count, latency, error), storage latency, and inter-node replication lag.

---

## When to consider this approach

- If your application proxies heavy file- or CPU-bound operations through a general-purpose web stack and you observe long-tail latencies or CPU starvation, extract the work into a specialized service exposed via RPC.  
- If stateful objects (repos, blobs) need HA and locality, add a replication/proxy layer that knows about object ownership.

---

## Visual summary

- Problem: Rails + git IO = contention and long tails.  
- Solution: Gitaly (specialized git RPC service) + Praefect (replication & routing) = lower latency, better scaling.
keyTakeaways: Separate heavy I/O/CPU work from your web layer — a specialized service (Gitaly) can reduce p99 RPC latency by multiple-fold and free Rails CPU.
Use a proxy/coordination layer (Praefect) for transparent replication and routing so clients don’t need to know about replica topology.
Invest in observability and testing for replication: metrics on RPC types, latency histograms, and replication lag are essential to keep HA safe.
relatedTopics: Replication and high-availability for stateful services

concept.title: Flip the Circuit, Not the System: Practical Circuit Breaker Pattern
concept.summary: When one downstream dependency starts failing or slowing, it can drag your whole system down. The circuit breaker pattern isolates failures, stops cascading load, and lets systems fail fast and recover gracefully.
keyTakeaways: Circuit breakers stop cascading failures by short-circuiting requests to unhealthy dependencies.
Tune thresholds, timeouts, and minimum throughput to avoid flapping and pointless opens.
Combine with fallbacks, retries (carefully), and observability to get resilient, debuggable behavior.
